title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Argentina — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Café is for quick standing visits
items.text: In Argentina, café means espresso—short, strong, and drunk standing at the counter in one or two gulps. If you want filter coffee or a long black, ask for 'café americano', though locals will find it unusual.

items.label: Cortado is the everyday drink
items.text: A cortado (espresso cut with a splash of steamed milk) is how Argentines actually take coffee throughout the day. It's cheap, quick, and the default order if you're unsure.

items.label: Medialunas with your coffee
items.text: These are Argentine croissants—buttery, flaky, and either sweet (dulce) or savory (salada with ham and cheese). They're the standard breakfast accompaniment and cost almost nothing.

items.label: Sit down costs more
items.text: Ordering at the counter (de pie) is significantly cheaper than sitting at a table (sentarse). The same cortado might be 50% more if you sit—this is completely normal and expected.

items.label: Café Tortoni (Buenos Aires)
items.text: A historic institution since 1858, full of old-world charm and tourists, but worth experiencing once. Expect to pay premium prices, but the atmosphere and history justify it.

items.label: Asado
items.text: Argentine barbecue featuring grilled beef ribs, chopped steak (bife), and offal cooked over an open fire. This is the national obsession—meat-centric, generous, and best shared with others at a long lunch or dinner.

items.label: Milanesa
items.text: Breaded and fried thin cutlet of beef, chicken, or sometimes fish, served with lemon and often with fries or salad. It's humble, ubiquitous, and Argentina's comfort food.

items.label: Empanadas
items.text: Baked or fried pastries filled with seasoned meat, cheese, or vegetables—the regional variations are enormous. Try them from street vendors, bakeries, and restaurants; each region claims theirs are the best.

items.label: Choripán
items.text: A grilled chorizo sausage in a crusty bread roll, often topped with chimichurri (parsley and garlic sauce). This is Argentina's street food staple and costs just a few pesos.

items.label: Provoleta
items.text: Sliced provolone cheese grilled until melted and slightly charred, served on a hot plate with chimichurri and crusty bread. It's a classic appetizer at asados and steakhouses.

items.label: Dulce de leche
items.text: Caramel made by slow-cooking sweetened milk until thick and spreadable—eaten on pastries, in desserts, or straight from a spoon. It's Argentina's unofficial national condiment and obsession.

items.label: Entradas are small plates, not first courses
items.text: In Argentine menus, 'entrada' means a small appetizer plate, not the main course as in English. Don't confuse it; order several to share before mains, or skip them if you want to go straight to the meat.

items.label: Menu del día is your best value
items.text: Lunch-time set menus (typically noon to 3pm) offer a fixed price for soup/salad, main, and dessert—often 30-40% cheaper than ordering à la carte. This is how locals eat.

items.label: Bife usually means ribeye, not just any steak
items.text: When you see 'bife' on the menu, it refers to a specific cut similar to ribeye. Other cuts are labeled differently (lomo for tenderloin, costilla for ribs, etc.), so know what you're ordering.

items.label: Ask how large the portion is
items.text: Portions in Argentine restaurants can be enormous—a single main dish often feeds two people. Don't hesitate to ask the server or order half-portions if available.

items.label: Bring your own wine in casual spots
items.text: Many restaurants, especially casual parrillas (grills), allow you to bring your own wine bottle with no corkage fee. This is not rude—it's expected in neighborhood spots and can save you money.

items.label: Parrilla (steakhouse/grill)
items.text: The working heart of Argentine dining—casual, loud, meat-focused, and excellent value. Go for lunch or arrive after 9pm for dinner; don't expect fancy presentation, just perfectly grilled meat.

items.label: Avoid obvious tourist traps on Caminito
items.text: The colorful streets of La Boca attract tourists with inflated prices and mediocre food. Walk a few blocks inland or choose parrillas frequented by locals for better food at fair prices.

items.label: Almacén or bodegón (neighborhood eatery)
items.text: These family-run, casual spots serve home-style Argentine food at rock-bottom prices. They're where locals eat, often with no English menu or signage—this is a feature, not a bug.

items.label: Pizzería
items.text: Argentine pizza is thick, cheesy, and excellent—quite different from Italian or American styles. Grab a slice from a stand or a whole pie at a casual pizzería in the evening (after 8pm).

items.label: San Telmo and La Boca markets on weekends
items.text: Street markets and food stalls offer fresh empanadas, choripanes, and grilled meat at vendor prices. These are authentic, cheap, and where you'll see real Buenos Aires life.

items.label: Book fine dining ahead
items.text: High-end parrillas and restaurants in Buenos Aires, especially in San Isidro or Belgrano, fill up quickly and require reservations. Call ahead or use a reservation app like TheFork.

items.label: Argentina is a wine country—order local
items.text: Argentine wine is world-class and inexpensive compared to Europe. Malbec is the national pride, but try Cabernet Sauvignon, Bonarda, and white Torrontés—you'll get excellent quality at 300-600 pesos.

items.label: Malbec from Mendoza
items.text: Dark, full-bodied, and pairs perfectly with grilled meat—this is Argentina's signature red wine. Even modest bottles are excellent; splurge a little and taste a quality Mendoza Malbec.

items.label: Fernet and cola (Fernet Branca)
items.text: Fernet mixed with cola is Argentina's spirit and national drink, especially among younger locals and late-night crowds. It's an acquired taste—herbal, bitter, and strong.

items.label: Agua mineral is standard
items.text: Still water (agua sin gas) or sparkling (agua con gas) is served at every meal and is very cheap. Ask for tap water (agua de la canilla) if you want free water, though it's less common than bringing bottled.

items.label: Quilmes beer
items.text: The most common Argentine lager—light, crisp, and refreshing. It's the default beer order and pairs well with asados and casual meals.

items.label: Wine is cheaper than beer or soda
items.text: Due to Argentina's wine production, a glass of quality wine at a restaurant often costs less than imported beer or soft drinks. Take advantage of this when ordering.

items.label: Dinner starts at 8:30pm minimum
items.text: Restaurants don't get busy until 9 or 9:30pm—eating before 8pm is considered early. If you arrive for dinner at 7pm, expect to find yourself nearly alone; go for an early coffee instead.

items.label: Lunch is the main meal (1pm–3pm)
items.text: Argentines eat a large midday meal and a lighter dinner. If you want the full restaurant experience and best value, eat lunch; many places have special lunch menus unavailable at dinner.

items.label: Don't expect speedy service
items.text: Argentine dining is leisurely—meals take 2-3 hours, and waiters won't rush you. This is not rudeness; it's culture. If you're in a hurry, eat at a café or casual spot standing up.

items.label: Chimichurri is always free
items.text: At parrillas and steakhouses, chimichurri (parsley, garlic, oil sauce) is served free as a condiment—it's your right as a diner. Use it generously on bread and meat.

items.label: Tip 10% for good service
items.text: Tipping is not obligatory but 10% for good service is customary and appreciated. In casual spots, rounding up the bill or leaving loose change is fine; in fine dining, leave 10-15%.

items.label: Share generously
items.text: Argentine dining culture is communal—meals are meant to be shared, especially asados and entradas. Don't be surprised if dishes are large enough for two or if locals suggest splitting plates.

items.label: Choripán
items.text: Grilled chorizo in crusty bread with chimichurri—the quintessential Argentine street food, available from street vendors everywhere. It costs 200-400 pesos and is a complete meal.

items.label: Facturas (sweet pastries)
items.text: Croissants, donuts, and cream-filled pastries sold at every bakery and café. Buy them fresh in the morning with your cortado for breakfast—they're often warm and delicious.

items.label: Medialunas
items.text: Sweet or savory croissants sold at bakeries (panaderías) throughout the day. They're cheap, fresh, and the go-to snack or light breakfast.

items.label: Panaderías (neighborhood bakeries)
items.text: Every block has a local bakery selling fresh bread, pastries, and empanadas at minimal cost. These are where Argentines get their breakfast and snacks—quality is generally excellent and prices rock-bottom.

items.label: Helado (ice cream)
items.text: Argentine ice cream is rich, creamy, and excellent—especially from specialized heladerías. Try flavors like dulce de leche or flan; a single scoop costs 300-500 pesos.
